title: Super Cappuccino: Scam, Lethal, or Just Misunderstood?
description: The super cappuccino debate is tearing breeder communities apart. Here's what the incomplete-dominant genetics actually say—and what responsible breeders do with that information.
tldr: Cappuccino is a proven incomplete-dominant morph — the super (frappuccino) form exists, but its viability is genuinely debated.
Cappuccino × cappuccino pairings risk producing frappuccinos with reduced clutch size or developmental issues; the data is incomplete.
The "scam" framing is wrong, but the caution behind it is legitimate — document outcomes before chasing super forms.
The safest Cappuccino pairing is always cappuccino × non-carrier: 50% visual cappuccino, zero lethal-super risk.
Buying a frappuccino from a line with no documented viability history is a gamble. Ask for clutch records before paying premium prices.

faq.question: Is super cappuccino (frappuccino) a legitimate morph or a scam?
faq.answer: Frappuccino is a legitimate morph — the homozygous expression of the incomplete-dominant cappuccino gene. It follows predictable Mendelian ratios. The "scam" label comes from undisclosed health concerns and inflated prices, not from the genetics being fake.

faq.question: What is the difference between cappuccino and frappuccino in crested geckos?
faq.answer: Cappuccino carries one copy of the allele (heterozygous); frappuccino carries two copies (homozygous). Cappuccino shows the characteristic dark dorsal saddle pattern; frappuccino shows a more extreme version of that pattern with a paler, more neutral body tone.

faq.question: Are frappuccino crested geckos healthy?
faq.answer: It depends on the line. Some breeders report healthy, long-lived frappuccinos. Others report reduced clutch viability, slower growth, and shorter lifespans. The data isn't yet sufficient to make a blanket claim either way. Ask for clutch records before buying.

faq.question: Is it safe to breed two cappuccino crested geckos together?
faq.answer: It isn't confirmed dangerous, but it isn't recommended without a plan to document outcomes. A cappuccino × cappuccino pairing produces 25% frappuccino, 50% cappuccino, and 25% non-carrier. The frappuccino viability is debated. Cappuccino × normal is the conservative default.

faq.question: How is frappuccino different from Super Lilly White?
faq.answer: Super Lilly White is a confirmed embryonic lethal — those eggs don't hatch. Frappuccino is a sublethal concern: the animals hatch and live, but some show health issues. Frappuccino belongs in the "breed cautiously and document" category, not the "never breed" category.

faq.question: What ratio of frappuccinos should I expect from a cappuccino × cappuccino pairing?
faq.answer: Approximately 25% frappuccino, 50% visual cappuccino, and 25% non-carrier — the standard incomplete-dominant 1:2:1 ratio. Punnett squares apply because cappuccino is a single-gene Mendelian morph.

faq.question: Why do frappuccino crested geckos cost so much?
faq.answer: Frappuccinos are rare because producing them requires pairing two visual cappuccinos, a pairing most cautious breeders avoid. Rarity plus the novelty of the super form drives prices to $1,500–$3,500. Whether the health risk justifies that premium depends on the specific line's documented history.

faq.question: How can I verify a frappuccino's genetics before buying?
faq.answer: Ask for documented parentage showing both parents were confirmed visual cappuccinos, clutch records showing egg-to-hatch outcomes, and follow-up data on prior frappuccinos from the same line at 12 months. A seller who cannot provide these is asking you to take their word alone.
